verbatim_text: And for the reward part, roughly we're looking at some intermediate progress that's measured through some of these process rewards. And in this particular case, we looked at using LLM judges equipped with the right proof rubrics in order to measure some kind of intermediate progress on some of these proofs. Now, in addition to these two quantities here, which define our training objective, we ran into a bunch of issues in the optimization part. Remember, I skipped some of the optimizer details, but that's also very important, in some cases even more important. And there are various knobs that need to be tuned in order to make the optimization stable. Typically on these hard problems, you end up generating very long traces with a lot of tokens that can make optimization hard, especially in cases where the reward is pretty sparse, your variance of gradients is high, and whatnot. So there are a bunch of these things that happen in practice. And we took our time in figuring out some of these details on what is the way to train. We look at curriculum over problems. We also look at how you can initialize problems with some hints or partial solutions. There are different techniques that you use, and there are a few others which I can go into offline. And when you use this, you do get a model that's better in the sense that, okay, it's improving performance matching that of 120B and 235B, but is it a model that adapts at test time? And as it turns out, if you take this QDNano model, we apply the scaffold on top of it, which is the DeepSeq math scaffold. It's a pretty standard scaffold.
